coredata.dc:title: NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/N-CNDs as a visible light photocatalyst for degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in water

coredata.dc:description: A microwave-assisted-hydrothermal approach has been employed to synthesize NH2-MIL-101(Fe) – (Fe-MOF) in composition with the Nitrogen-doped Carbon Nanodots - (N-CNDs) as a visible light photocatalyst. The octahedral structure of Fe-MOF was confirmed via microscopic and X-ray powder diffraction analysis. The composite with 30 % N-CNDs exhibited the highest degradation efficiency of 97.9 % for Ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. Also, it shows a higher photocurrent response than bare Fe-MOF material. Also, EIS analysis reveals a smaller arc for the composite material, which suggests higher charge transfer properties. Based on the transient photocurrent measurements and EIS analysis, it could be stated that the improved photocatalytic activity of Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite is due to the enhanced charge transfer properties caused by the introduction of N-CNDs. The photoactivity studies were tested using the LC-MS technique.

Carbon nanodot

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping study
Possible mechanism of photo-degradation of CIP using Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Scanning Electron Microscopic (SEM) images were taken to elucidate the morphological characteristics. The microwave synthesis of N-CNDs resulted in the formation of irregularly shaped nanoparticles (Fig. 2 (a)), having an average size of less than 100 nm [42], shown in Fig. S5 (a). Meanwhile, the Fe-MOF exhibits a perfectly shaped octahedron geometry (Fig. 2 (b)), with an average size of 391 nm (Fig. S6 (a)). This was in agreement with the previously reported articles [40,43]. From the SEM image of the Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite (Fig. 2 (c)), the integration between the Fe-MOF nanoparticles and the N-CNDs surface could be clearly observed. The particle size of the photocatalytic material plays a significant role in enhancing its efficiency. The size distribution investigation reveals that the size of Fe-MOF crystals (162 nm) in the composite material was smaller than in the bare Fe-MOF, Fig. 2 (d). This indicates that the introduction of N-CNDs affects the formation of Fe-MOF during the reaction process [44]. This further confirms the bonding of N-CNDs with the Fe-MOF nanoparticles, which supports the efficient charge transfer process [45]. Usually, as the particle size decreases, the photocatalytic rate constants increase exponentially [46]. It is usually attributed to the availability of the larger active sites and results in the rapid charge transfer process [47]. This agrees with the enhanced photo efficiency of composite material compared to the larger size bare Fe-MOF. Cheng. et al. also demonstrated that the photocatalytic efficiency could be elevated with the decrease in the size of AgI nanoparticles [48]. The corresponding EDX data (Fig. S7) and the mapping images (Fig. 2(e\u2013i)) confirm the presence and distribution of C, N, O, and Fe elements in the Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite. Similarly, the EDS analysis and the corresponding mapping images reveal the distribution of (C, N, & O), and (C, N, O, & Fe) elements in N-CNDs (Fig. S5 (b-f)) and Fe-MOF (Fig. S6 (b-g)). Table S1 demonstrates the elemental percentage in each photocatalyst analyzed from the EDX analysis. The EDX spectra of Fe-MOF and the composite also reveal the presence of a minute quantity of chloride element sourced from the iron chloride used in the Fe-MOF synthesis. This might be because chloride ions participate in the coordination process along with the iron sites in the synthesis process [39].N. Kaushal and S. head.ce:title: NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/N-CNDs as a visible light photocatalyst for degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A microwave-assisted-hydrothermal approach has been employed to synthesize NH2-MIL-101(Fe) – (Fe-MOF) in composition with the Nitrogen-doped Carbon Nanodots - (N-CNDs) as a visible light photocatalyst. The octahedral structure of Fe-MOF was confirmed via microscopic and X-ray powder diffraction analysis. The composite with 30 % N-CNDs exhibited the highest degradation efficiency of 97.9 % for Ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. Also, it shows a higher photocurrent response than bare Fe-MOF material. Also, EIS analysis reveals a smaller arc for the composite material, which suggests higher charge transfer properties. Based on the transient photocurrent measurements and EIS analysis, it could be stated that the improved photocatalytic activity of Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite is due to the enhanced charge transfer properties caused by the introduction of N-CNDs. The photoactivity studies were tested using the LC-MS technique.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the widespread increase in the human population, an upsurge in antibiotic consumption has been observed globally [1]. Annually, the antibiotics market is increasing production from 1k to 2k USD tons worldwide [2]. This poses harmful effects not only to aquatic life but also makes drinking water unfit for consumers if left untreated [3]. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), with a 30 mg/mL solubility, is the most prescribed among the family of fluoroquinolone antibiotics. It is generally considered non-biodegradable, and its extensive environmental presence risks the health of animals and humans [4]. Different physical, chemical, and biological methods, including reverse osmosis [5], ultrafiltration, and electrocoagulation [6], are employed to remove CIP from water. However, most of these methods are ineffective due to the requirement for large amounts of energy and capital investments, and they might lead to toxic byproducts [2].

Photocatalysis has been considered a reliable method among Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) for effectively removing antibiotics in water [7]. It uses a light-active catalyst to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). These charge carriers further react with the pollutant, converting it into less harmful products [8]. Since solar energy consists of only 3 % of the UV region and also UV photons exhibit high energy that may cause an unproductive decomposition reaction [9], the need is to develop visible light (400–800 nm) active photocatalysts. Generally, a catalyst with relatively less toxicity, high chemical stability, facile synthesis, high light absorption, and effective charge separation is considered an ideal photocatalyst [8].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a family of porous materials, mainly consisting of clusters of metal ions and the organic ligands attached to them [10]. These materials possess promising properties to be used as photocatalysts, including sizeable specific surface area, variable topology, and high adsorption of reactant molecules, thus facilitating the availability of large active sites. Among the class of MOFs, Fe-based MOFs are gaining worldwide attention among environmentalists due to their exceptional characteristics [11]. These usually consist of Fe (III) ions with coordination bonds with oxygen atoms, acting as Lewis acids, while organic ligands work as Lewis bases. Although having exceptional properties, employing these materials for photocatalysis comes with the challenges [12] of low light responsiveness, inadequate rate of charge transfer, and low conductivity. One strategy to enhance these properties is to prepare heterojunctions of MOFs with a suitable semiconductor to ensure the separation of charge carriers and the effective removal of pollutants. C. Huang et al. [13], have demonstrated the coupling of V2O5 with NH2-MIL-101(Fe) for the improved photodegradation of tetracycline antibiotics under UV–Vis light illumination. Similarly, another recent report by H. Shi et al. [14], has reported the composition of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) with BiVO4 to remove nitrate contamination from water and convert it into N2. Y. Li et al. demonstrated the synthesis of mesoporous Zn/Co-ZIFs@MIL-101(Fe) composites for the photo-Fenton removal of Rhodamine B dye under visible light illumination [15].
However, the use of heavy metals or external reagents like H2O2, etc., further harms the environment if employed in large quantities.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), which range in size from nanometers to millimeters [16], are well-known for their appealing properties like relatively low toxicity and environmental compatibility [17], optical and electronic characteristics [18], and charge transfer properties [19]. Solar light illumination can activate these organic semiconductors to generate photo-charge carriers [20]. Additionally, they can act as excellent electron donors and acceptors [21,22], making them widely applicable in photocatalysis. Moreover, the photoactivity of CNDs could be further enhanced by the introduction of heteroatom doping. Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots are reported to have better charge transfer properties, thereby improving photocatalytic performance [23,24]. This study proposes that the composition of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots - (N-CNDs) with NH2-MIL-101(Fe) – (Fe-MOF) could highly enhance the visible light photocatalytic performance of Fe-MOF for effective removal of organic pollutants from water. We believe that the model organic pollutant (Ciprofloxacin) used in our study sufficiently demonstrates the catalytic performance of Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs. Also, this approach is widely accepted and has been utilized by numerous research groups to evaluate similar catalysts [15,25]. The model pollutant provides valuable insights into the material's catalytic mechanism, reactivity, and efficiency, which can be reasonably extended to other contaminants like dyes.

Heterojunction formation significantly enhances visible light absorption and, thus, the performance of the photocatalysts for contaminants degradation in water [26]. Most bare semiconductor photocatalysts like Fe-MOF show low photo efficiency due to the fast recombination of the photoexcited charge carriers. This leads to less availability of photo-excited charge carriers for utilization in photocatalysis [27]. If the two semiconducting materials having different fermi energy levels are coupled together, the electric field gets built in at the interface of the heterojunction by the diffusion of charges between the semiconductors [28]. The construction of heterojunctions by the composition of two semiconducting materials effectively improves the spatial separation of photo-excited charges. Carbon nanodots help to improve the charge mobility and, hence, the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite material. Thus, in this study, the nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots are coupled with the amine-activated Fe-MOF nanomaterials to enhance the overall photocatalytic efficiency for visible light degradation of CIP.

Previously documented studies demonstrate carbon nanodots' role in enhancing heterostructure efficiency. For instance, X. Liu et al. have reported the S-doped Fe2O3/NC/Fe-MOF heterostructure to test the photocatalytic performance for electrochemical oxygen evolution [29]. R. Zhao et al. reported the photo removal of tetracycline antibiotic using NH2 functionalized MOF, UiO-66@Polysulfone porous material in composition with CQDs under UV–visible light illumination [30]. Also, Q. Ye et al. investigated the role of incorporating fluorescent CQDs into MOFs for peroxide-mimicking activities [31]. Additionally, R. Zhao et al. have highlighted the role of charge transfer properties of CQDs in enhancing the photoefficiency of UiO-66-NH2 by studying composites of how polysulfone microspheres with CDs and MOF material for the photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) [32]. Similarly, Z. Wang et al. demonstrated the hybrid nanoparticles containing MIL-101(Fe) in composition with CNC (cellulose nanocrystals) for the photo-Fenton removal of Rhodamine B dye under visible light irradiation [33]. However, hardly any report has demonstrated the use of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots in composition with NH2-modified Fe-MOF for visible light degradation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic in water. This study suggests that the excellent charge transfer properties of N-CNDs could prevent the fast charge recombination in Fe-MOF, thus improving its visible light photoactivity. K. Bian et al. suggested using CQDs decorated MIL-101(Fe) composite for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline [34]. However, it involves the laborious multi-step synthesis approach to fabricate the final products.

In this study, N-CNDs were synthesized using a solid-phase microwave synthesis process, which offers advantages in speed and uniformity over other methods. The composites of Fe-MOF/N-CNDs with varying loading percentages of N-CNDs (10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and 50 %) were fabricated, and their photodegradation efficiency was systematically investigated under visible light illumination using ciprofloxacin antibiotic as an organic model. Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite possesses the highest photocatalytic activity for photo-degradation of ciprofloxacin in water, with a removal rate of 97.9 %. The increase in the BET-specific surface area and the improved transient photocurrent response on the addition of N-CNDs validate the highest photo efficiency of composite material. Based on the scavenger studies and Mott-Schottky analysis, it has been proposed that holes and hydroxyl radicals play a vital role in removing ciprofloxacin using Fe-MOF/N-CNDs composites.

Citric Acid (>99.5 %), Isopropyl alcohol (>99.7 %), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (Average M.W. 10,000), 2-amino terephthalic acid (99 %), Nafion (5 %), and Methanol (99 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich; Urea was obtained from Fisher Chemicals. N, N-Dimethylformamide (99.7 %), and Iron (III) Chloride Hexahydrate (97 %) were purchased from Alfa Aesar.

Citric acid (CA) and Urea (U) were used as carbon and nitrogen sources to synthesize N-CNDs. The initial precursor's ratio was maintained as 1:3 for CA: U as per our previously reported work [21]. Briefly, the powdered mixture was crushed homogeneously using pestle mortar and heated at 250 °C for 30 min in a microwave synthesis reactor (BP-125, S. No. SN230410123H09). The solid black-colored product obtained was extracted and again crushed uniformly using pestle mortar, followed by purification of obtained N-CNDs using our previously documented approach [23]. After purification, the residual powder was dried overnight at 40 °C under vacuum conditions. The as-synthesized product is designated as N-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe-MOF and composites of Fe-MOF/N-CNDs

ce:section.ce:para: Here, aminated NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was synthesized using a hydrothermal approach using our previously adopted method [35]. Previous reports suggest that the aminated Fe-MOF exhibits better light-absorbing properties for a wider wavelength and can also elevate the rate of the electron transfer process [35]. Briefly, 1.242 mmol of 2-amino-terephthalic acid (acts as an amination linker) was dissolved in 7.5 mL of DMF in container A. Then, 2.497 mmol of FeCl3.6H2O was dissolved in 7.5 mL of DMF separately in container B. After this, the above-prepared solutions were mixed and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Further, 0.1 g of PVP was added to the solution, and stirring was repeated for 30 min. The solution mixture was then transferred to the 50 mL stainless Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 110 °C for 24 h. The dark brown product was centrifugated (7000 rpm, 15 min) and washed with DMF and methanol several times. The residue obtained was dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C, and the obtained product NH2-MIL-101(Fe) is designated as Fe-MOF.

A similar approach was followed for the synthesis of Fe-MOF@x%N-CNDs composites. The different weight percentages of N-CNDs (10, 20, 30, and 50)% were added to the mixture solution of A and B, as mentioned in section 2.3.1. The solution was sonicated for 20 min after adding N-CNDs to ensure homogeneity. The solution obtained is then transferred to a 50 mL stainless Teflon-lined autoclave and heated under similar aforementioned conditions. The composites were separated by centrifugation (7000 rpm, 15 min), followed by washing and drying overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The final products obtained possess light brown to dark brown coloration. These products are designated as Fe-MOF@10%N-CNDs, Fe-MOF@20%N-CNDs, Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs, and Fe-MOF@50%N-CNDs. The detailed synthesis process and the images of samples obtained are demonstrated in Figs. S1 and S2.

The visible light photocatalysis experiments were conducted using a 300 W Xe Lamp (420 nm cut-off filter). The ambient temperature condition was maintained during experimentation by a water cooling system. Initially, 18 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in the 30 mL aqueous solution of 0.01 mg/mL CIP with constant stirring throughout the experiment. 1 mL aliquot of the reaction solution was extracted every 30 min and centrifugated, followed by filtration (using a 0.2 μm Nylon syringe filter). The reaction solution was kept in the dark for 30 min to establish equilibrium before light illumination. The height between the reaction solution and the lamp was 7 cm. The obtained samples were tested using an LC-MS technique and analyzed using Mass Hunter software. The calibration curve was prepared using different concentration stock solutions of CIP (Fig. S3 (a)). The corresponding Counts (%) vs. Time plot and chromatograph for CIP are also shown in Fig. S3 (b & c). The photocatalytic efficiency was checked by measuring the CIP removal rate (R%) and the photo-degradation rate (D%) using the following equations;

Various spectroscopic and morphological analysis methods were employed to characterize the prepared photocatalysts. The Powder X-ray Diffraction (P-XRD) analysis of the samples was performed using a PANalytical Xpert Pro diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The structural and morphological analysis was done using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) by FEI Aprio LV-SEM. Further, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using FEI CM20 TEM. For sample preparation, the catalysts were dispersed in the isopropyl alcohol and sonicated for 30 min before drop casting on the Carbon–Copper grid. ImageJ software was used to calculate the size distribution. The Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area analysis was performed by N2 adsorption on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 instrument. The samples were oven-dried at 120 °C for 6 h before analysis. The elemental composition analysis was done by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipped with Al/Mg X-ray twin source (1253.6 eV MgKα and 1486.6 eV AlKα). The percentage composition of each element in the prepared photocatalysts was studied using XPSCasa software. The functional groups analysis was done using Nicolet 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer (FTIR) from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The photo-degradation efficiency was tested using the Agilent technologies, 6540 UHD Accurate-Mass Q-TOF LC/MS technique with Agilent ZORBAX Eclipse Plus C18 Column (4.6 × 50 mm, 1.8 μm). The flow rate of 0.40 mL/min was maintained with an injection volume of 5 μL. 95 % of solvent A (Water in 0.1 % Formic Acid) and 5 % of Solvent B (Acetonitrile in 0.1 % Formic Acid) were used to analyze CIP concentration.

Powder X-ray Diffraction was performed to understand the structure and atomic composition of the photocatalysts (Fig. 1 ). In particular, the well-defined characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ values, 5.8°, 8.9°, 10.3°, 16.4°, 18.7°, and 24.5° corresponding to (111), (220), (311), (002), (511), and (852) reflections [37] aligns well with the Fd3m structure, confirming the formation of bare NH2-MIL-101(Fe), and is consistent with the previously documented reports [38–40]. The microwave synthesis of (N-CNDs) resulted in the formation of the crystalline phase. The characteristic peak at 2θ value 27.5° reveals the interlayer stacking of crystalline planes corresponding to the (002) reflections [23]. The XRD patterns of Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst show the typical peaks of N-CNDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) with minor variations, suggesting the successful formation of the composites. However, the intensity of the characteristic peak of N-CNDs was relatively low. This could be due to the low concentration of N-CNDs relative to the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) in the composite that results in the desolation of the interlayer arrangement of N-CNDs in the reaction process, as suggested in other reported studies [41]. Similar results were seen in the diffraction patterns of the Fe-MOF@x%N-CNDs (x = 10, 20, and 50) depicted in Fig. S4. The increase in the efficiency of composite material could be attributed to the presence of lattice defects introduced by N-CNDs into Fe-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping study
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning Electron Microscopic (SEM) images were taken to elucidate the morphological characteristics. The microwave synthesis of N-CNDs resulted in the formation of irregularly shaped nanoparticles (Fig. 2 (a)), having an average size of less than 100 nm [42], shown in Fig. S5 (a). Meanwhile, the Fe-MOF exhibits a perfectly shaped octahedron geometry (Fig. 2 (b)), with an average size of 391 nm (Fig. S6 (a)). This was in agreement with the previously reported articles [40,43]. From the SEM image of the Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite (Fig. 2 (c)), the integration between the Fe-MOF nanoparticles and the N-CNDs surface could be clearly observed. The particle size of the photocatalytic material plays a significant role in enhancing its efficiency. The size distribution investigation reveals that the size of Fe-MOF crystals (162 nm) in the composite material was smaller than in the bare Fe-MOF, Fig. 2 (d). This indicates that the introduction of N-CNDs affects the formation of Fe-MOF during the reaction process [44]. This further confirms the bonding of N-CNDs with the Fe-MOF nanoparticles, which supports the efficient charge transfer process [45]. Usually, as the particle size decreases, the photocatalytic rate constants increase exponentially [46]. It is usually attributed to the availability of the larger active sites and results in the rapid charge transfer process [47]. This agrees with the enhanced photo efficiency of composite material compared to the larger size bare Fe-MOF. Cheng. et al. also demonstrated that the photocatalytic efficiency could be elevated with the decrease in the size of AgI nanoparticles [48]. The corresponding EDX data (Fig. S7) and the mapping images (Fig. 2(e–i)) confirm the presence and distribution of C, N, O, and Fe elements in the Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite. Similarly, the EDS analysis and the corresponding mapping images reveal the distribution of (C, N, & O), and (C, N, O, & Fe) elements in N-CNDs (Fig. S5 (b-f)) and Fe-MOF (Fig. S6 (b-g)). Table S1 demonstrates the elemental percentage in each photocatalyst analyzed from the EDX analysis. The EDX spectra of Fe-MOF and the composite also reveal the presence of a minute quantity of chloride element sourced from the iron chloride used in the Fe-MOF synthesis. This might be because chloride ions participate in the coordination process along with the iron sites in the synthesis process [39].

TEM images were taken to further understand the structure of the as-synthesized photocatalysts Fig. 3 (a–c). For Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs, it is observed that Fe-MOF is well distributed on the surface of N-CNDs, which is consistent with the SEM results. Although the Fe-MOF crystals have perfect octahedral geometry, which aligns well with the previous results [45]. The coupling of N-CNDs with the Fe-MOF is observed, as highlighted in Fig. 3(c). However, its morphology is modified in the heterostructure as irregular particles with smaller size distribution. This indicates the interactions existing between N-CNDs and Fe-MOF in the heterostructure, thus confirming the composite formation.

The relationship between the surface area and photoactivity is well established, where higher surface area values often lead to increased photoactivity due to more active sites available for reactions. Therefore, the BET analysis not only provides insights into surface characteristics like pore size and volume but also serves as a valuable indicator of the photoactivity potential of nanomaterials, crucial for various applications requiring efficient surface interactions.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves for all the samples exhibit Type I adsorption (Fig. 4 (a–f)). This reveals the microporous nature of the Fe-MOF and its composites with the N-CNDs [49]. The surface area of Fe-MOF and N-CNDs was measured as 66.7 m2/g and 49.1 m2/g, respectively. However, its composite, Fe-MOF-@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst, depicts the highest BET-specific surface area value of 676.6 m2/g, which explains its highest photoactivity for the degradation of CIP. A similar change in the BET surface has been observed by previous literature reports as well [50,51]. This increase in the surface area of the composite material could be due to the formation of nanostructured material, as the particle size obtained from SEM images of composite material is measured to be less than bare Fe-MOF. Since nanomaterials with smaller particle sizes expose more surface area, the total surface area is drastically increased. Also, the porosity of the material plays an important role. The porous nature of the N-CNDs might have introduced pores between N-CNDs and Fe-MOF, increasing the surface area of the composite. The higher surface area accounts for the availability of more active sites, thus elevating the photo efficiency of the nanomaterials. This is observed in the trend of photoactivity of prepared nanomaterials as below; Fe-MOF@30 % N-CNDs (676.6 m2/g) > Fe-MOF@50 % N-CNDs (537.7 m2/g) > Fe-MOF@20 % N-CNDs (380.3 m2/g) > Fe-MOF@10 % N-CNDs (92.4 m2/g) > Fe-MOF (66.7 m2/g).

the increased photo efficiency of the nanomaterials. Further, the pore size distributions are represented in (Fig. S8 (a-f) and S9 (a-f)) for all the prepared nanomaterials. These curves consist of sharp peaks with varying values ranging between 1 and 10 nm for all the materials. The BJH (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) pore size distribution curves were obtained to calculate the pore size and volume values, as depicted in Table S2.
Thus, the relationship between surface area and photoactivity explains the increasing trend of photo efficiency of nanomaterials.

The elemental composition and the corresponding surface electronic states was further confirmed by XPS analysis. The full-scale spectra of N-CNDs, Fe-MOF, and Fe-MOF@30 % N-CNDs are represented in Fig. 5 (a). These spectra verify that Fe-MOF and its composite mainly consist of C, N, O, and Fe, which is consistent with the EDX results. The corresponding atomic percentage values obtained for each material are represented in Table 1 . For Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst, the deconvoluted spectra of C (1s) Fig. 5 (b) represent three peaks at 285.2 eV, 286.8 eV, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to the C–C50, C–N–C bonds, CN bonds, and carboxylate group (O–C–O) groups [52] respectively. However, these peaks occurred at 283.5 eV, 284.6 eV, and 287.4 eV in the XPS spectra of bare Fe-MOF (Fig. S10 (a)). This shift in the energy in the composite material indicates the variation in the electron density for the C atom due to the presence of N-CNDs in the composite. Also, the presence of C–N and CN bonds plays a crucial role in strengthening electron mobility due to the presence of electron-rich nitrogen sites that facilitate the effective charge transfer within the heterojunction [53].

The N (1s) spectrum in Fig. 5 (c) displays two deconvoluted peaks at 399.04 eV and 400.12 eV, which can be attributed to the formation of the N–H bond in the NH2-BDC ligand [43] and N-HX bond [52]. However, in comparison to the XPS spectrum of N(1s) in Fe-MOF (Fig. S10 (b)), a positive shift in the 398.5 eV peak, which corresponds to the C–N bond, is observed in the composite material. This could be related to the interactions between the N-CNDs and Fe-MOF as the N-atom contains a lone pair of electrons in Fe-MOF, and carboxylic groups in the N-CNDs could participate in the reaction process [50]. Fig. (5 (d)) shows the deconvoluted XPS spectra of O (1s); the energy peaks at 532.9 eV and 531.9 eV confirm the cluster formation due to Fe–O and C–O bonds in the NH2-BDC respectively [39].

The high-resolution XPS spectrum for Fe (2p) (Fig. 5 (e)) consists of two prominent peaks located at 711.9 eV and 725.5 eV, attributed to the Fe (2p)1/2, and Fe (2p)3/2, respectively. Also, a satellite peak observed at 716.14 eV confirms the presence of Fe3+ ions. Notably, the two prominent peaks are separated by a distance of 13.6 eV, a characteristic feature [39] of alpha-Fe2O3. Similarly, there is a positive shift to 711.9 eV and 725.5 eV in Fe-MOF@30 % N-CNDs relative to the Fe-MOF (710.9 eV and 724.1 eV) oxidation peaks (Fig. S10 (d)).

This might be due to the changes in the chemical environment caused by the introduction of N-CNDs [45]. Thus, it confirms the successful formation of the composite material. Also, these improve the electron transfer from MOF to the pollutant by facilitating the photogenerated hole migration towards the N-CNDs, which is validated by the shift in the B.E. peaks in the composite material. This favors the possible explanation for the reaction mechanism as well. Thus, enhances the photocatalytic efficiency for the removal of Ciprofloxacin [54,55].The XPS spectra for N-CNDs are represented in the supporting information Fig. S11 (a-c).
The FTIR spectra for N-CNDs, Fe-MOF, and their composites Fe-MOF@x%N-CNDs (x = 10, 20, 30, & 50) were measured from 400 to 4000 cm−1 (Fig. 6 ). N-CNDs show a significant broad band in the range

from 2900 to 3200 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibrations associated with the N–H functional groups [21]. This is also observed in all the composite materials, demonstrating the co-existence of N-CNDs and Fe-MOF in the heterogeneous structures. Furthermore, the Fe-MOF sample also consists of typical characteristic bands of amino groups ranging from 3400 to 3300 cm−1 due to the N–H groups' asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations [52]. These become stronger in the composites due to the introduction of N-CNDs. Additionally, the Fe-MOF sample shows peaks at 1652 cm−1, 1580 cm−1, and 762 cm−1, responsible for the vibrations from δ (N–H), carboxylate groups (-COOH), and C–H groups in the benzene ring [43,51]. The bands from 1200 to 1600 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C–O–C bonds in the Fe-MOF. Moreover, the peaks in the range from 500 to 600 cm−1 represent the vibrations of the Fe–O clusters [56], which could be seen in all the composite samples. There is hardly any variation in the Fe–O bond intensity in the composite material, suggesting the composition of N-CNDs introduces the chemical bonding with Fe-MOF without altering its properties. These bonds help prevent the charge recombination process, which is also discussed in the XPS section.

The optical band gap calculations were performed using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. The aqueous solution of a certain concentration was prepared, and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (a). All the as-synthesized materials show strong absorption in the visible wavelength region, which could be ascribed to the presence of amino groups and Fe–O bonds [43]. However, a redshift has been observed in all the composites compared to bare Fe-MOF, along with a rise in absorbance. This further suggests the strong visible light absorption ability of Fe-MOF@ x% N-CNDs composites (x = 10, 20, 30, and 50). Similarly, the absorbance spectra of N-CNDs (Fig. S12 (a)) show visible light absorbance, which further suggests the role of N-CNDs in enhancing the visible light activity of Fe-MOF. Furthermore, the Kubelka-Munk equation [57] has been used to measure the band gap Eg of all the samples according to eq. (5);
eq. (5)
α h ν =
A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n / 2
where, α = absorption coefficient, hν = photon energy, Eg = band gap, A = constant, and n = 1 for direct band gap transitions. The Tauc plots have been obtained for each material, as shown in Fig. 7 (b), and the calculated band gap values are presented in Table 2 . The band gap value of Fe-MOF was measured to be 2.45 eV, and after composition with N-CNDs, the recorded value of Eg = 1.90 eV (Fig. S12 (b)) suggests a red shift in the band gap of composite materials. This decline in the band gap might be due to the formation of intermediate energy states at the heterojunction's interface [58]. The introduction of N-CNDs improves the visible light absorption properties of the Fe-MOF material. This is attributed to the formation of intermediate energy states due to nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots synthesized by the solid phase synthesis approach. Also, in our previous report [21,22] it was explained that nitrogen doping leads to the formation of a low-lying intermediate state between the energy bands that results in the π∗–π type energy transitions, resulting in red-shift interstate transitions. D. Qu et al. [59] also explained in detail that N-CNDs usually consist of π∗ orbital due to the formation of CO and CN bonds. The introduction of N leads to the red shift in the absorption and emission transitions due to these chemical bonds [58]. Thus, the composite material shows better light absorption with a lower energy. This adds to the prevention of charge recombination and thus enhances the photocatalytic efficiency of the material, as suggested by previously reported studies as well [23,60,61]. This is further explained by electrochemical analysis using EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy) and TPR (Transient Photocurrent Response) measurements. However, with the increase in the loading of N-CNDs above the optimum value of 30 %, it might be possible that the agglomeration of nanoparticles takes place, which hinders the penetration of light and thus decreases the photocatalytic efficiency for the Fe-MOF@50%N-CNDs composite [62]. Similar behavior has been observed in the literature reports as well [63].

EIS technique has been used to obtain Nyquist plots under visible light illumination. These mainly consist of two sections: a semi-circular arc followed by a linear region. Generally, the smaller the arc, the better the charge transfer properties and the higher the material's conductivity [64]. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) measurements are done using EIS Spectrum Analyzer software. The curve fitting is performed, considering the circuit equivalent to the Randles circuit [65] as shown in Fig. S13. The charge transfer resistance value for the composite material is calculated to be 74838 Ω with an error of 2.4 %, which is 86.92 % less than the value for the bare Fe-MOF (Rct = 5.72∗10^5, error = 3.8 %). It could be suggested that introducing N-CNDs increases photocatalytic efficiency by preventing electron-hole recombination in the composite material. The fitted curves and charge transfer resistance value for Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite in comparison with the bare FE-MOF are presented in Fig. S13. It can be seen from Fig. 8 (a) that Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs show a smaller arc region relative to Fe-MOF and N-CNDs. Thus, it possesses a higher conductivity and less electron-hole recombination, which contributes to improving its photocatalytic efficiency.

Fig. 8 (b) represents the photocurrent measurements done for Fe-MOF, N-CNDs, and Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs, under visible light illumination. The Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite material possesses a higher transient photocurrent value than the Fe-MOF and N-CNDs. It could be inferred that N-CNDs help improve the transfer of charge carriers generated under light irradiation. In our previous studies, the detailed time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) studies elucidated that N-CNDs exhibit the most extended lifetime with time constants of up to 10.06 ns. Also, our another previous research [23] on zinc ferrite/nitrogen-doped carbon dots composites demonstrates an increase in photo-efficiency of the composite material due to effective charge carrier properties of the N-CNDs. These improve electron mobility and extend charge carrier lifetimes in related composite systems; hence, they play a crucial role in enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency for removing CIP from water. [31,41,66]Thus, it could be illustrated that the substantial rise in photocurrent observed in Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs in this study is likely due to the enhanced charge separation and reduced charge recombination, often associated with nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs).

The current density is directly correlated with the concentration of charge carriers generated in the presence of light illumination. Thus, LSV measurements were conducted to test the charge carrier generation in the photocatalysts. The photocurrent density of Fe-MOF, N-CNDs, and Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs (Fig. 8 (c)) electrodes were measured as a function of applied voltage under visible light irradiation. Compared to Fe-MOF (0.19 mA/cm2), Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs have a higher current density (0.27 mA/cm2) under visible light illumination. According to these results, it could be stated that more charge carrier generation in Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs leads to better photocatalytic efficiency [67].

The photo-activity of all the as-synthesized materials has been tested by removing CIP antibiotics under visible light illumination. A 0.6 mg/mL catalyst was used to remove 0.01 mg/mL of CIP in neutral pH conditions. The reaction was performed in a 100 mL beaker placed under Xe Lamp (420 nm cut-off filter) under continuous stirring to maintain the homogeneity of the reaction solution. Initially, stirring was done for 30 min to obtain dark adsorption equilibrium, and then the reaction solution was exposed to visible light irradiation for the next 180 min.
The concentration versus time plots (Fig. 9 (a)) were obtained for all the samples. It was observed that the photo-degradation rate and the removal rate follow the trend as mentioned:
Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs > Fe-MOF@50%N-CNDs > Fe-MOF@20%N-CNDs > Fe-MOF@10%N-CNDs > Fe-MOF.

All the prepared photocatalysts follow the pseudo-first-order rate kinetics. The fittings curves for the first-order kinetics were obtained using the below equation;
eq. (6)

Here,

refers to the initial concentration of CIP after dark equilibrium and
C 0 ′
refers to the initial concentration of CIP before dark equilibrium. The removal rate constants and photo-degradation rate constant values (min−1) (Table 3 ) were obtained from the first-order rate kinetics plots (Fig. 9 (b and c)). The percentage decline in CIP concentration has been presented in Fig. 9(d). The corresponding
C t
/
C 0 ′
vs. time, and
C t
/
C 0
vs. time plots for the removal and photodegradation rates are presented in Fig S14(a & b). It has been investigated that Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst shows the best efficiency of 97.9 % removal of CIP in 180 min of light illumination, which was only 54.1 % for Fe-MOF. Thus, it could be inferred that the composition of Fe-MOF with N-CNDs enhances the photo-efficiency of the material, which is consistent with the characterization results obtained. Furthermore, a comparison of the photocatalytic performance of previously reported literature reports along with this work is demonstrated in Table S3. Additionally, the adsorption test was performed using 0.6 mg/mL of the best performing photocatalyst (Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs) in 10 ppm of CIP without any light illumination. The samples were collected after fixed intervals of time, and the plot obtained is included in the supporting information in Fig. S15. It could be concluded that although adsorption efficiency is high, adsorption equilibrium was attained. The light activation of the catalyst plays an important role in the overall removal of CIP in the photocatalysis process.

Further, to test the photo efficiency of the catalyst, preliminary tests of the best-performing catalyst for the photodegradation of Basic Blue dye (Cationic Dye) has been done using a UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Fig. S16). Results show that a 65.8 % decline in the dye (25 ppm) is observed using Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs (0.6 mg/mL) catalyst after 1 h of visible light illumination (Xe Lamp, 420 nm cut-off filter). However, detailed testing with specific pollutants like dyes or heavy metals could be explored in future studies to validate the broad applicability of this catalyst across different types of contaminants.

To demonstrate the photostability of the best-performing catalyst (Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs), a recyclability test was performed for four consecutive cycles. After each photocatalytic run (0.6 mg/mL catalyst, 0.01 mg/mL CIP, pH 7), the catalyst was recovered by centrifugation. The washing was done in D.I. water multiple times to remove the excess adsorbed pollutant, and drying was done in a vacuum oven at 40 °C for the next 24 h. Finally, the recovered catalyst was reused for photo-degradation of CIP for the next cycle. It could be seen that after four cycles, a minor decline in photo efficiency has been observed (Fig. S17). However, no significant changes are determined in the XPS analysis of recycled samples after four cycles in comparison (Fig. S18).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of photo-degradation of CIP using Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs

ce:section.ce:para: It is observed that the participation rates of hydroxyl radicals, electrons, and holes were 58.6 %, 54.3 %, and 60.3 %, respectively.

The Mott-Schottky (M − S) analysis was performed for Fe-MOF, N-CNDs, and Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs composite to further clarify the energy band potentials, shown in Fig. S 20(a-b) & S 21, respectively. These consist of a positive slope, which indicates all three materials are n-type semiconductors. The value of flat band potential has been obtained by extrapolating the M − S graph, where 1/Cs 2 equals zero. The M − S relation for n-type semiconductors is as follows;

Also, since the conduction band potential of an n-type semiconductor is 0.10 V lower than the flat band potential, the ECB value of Fe-MOF, N-CNDs, and Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs is calculated to be −0.14, −0.38, and −0.07 V vs NHE respectively. Further, the relation between the conduction band potential and valence band potential is applied to measure the EVB as follows:

Thus, the valence band potential values are obtained using the band gap values obtained from the Tauc plots in the above equation, as shown in Table 2.

Further, the LC-MS technique has been employed to understand the photo-degradation pathway of CIP under visible light illumination using Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst. The chromatographs have been obtained and analyzed to extract the possible intermediate products of CIP. The LC-MS tests have identified several oxidation products. The most dominant intermediate products are represented in Table S4, along with their plausible structures. This study suggests that most intermediate products (m/z = 362, 306, 260) are generated due to cleavage of the piperazine ring of (m/z = 332). The oxidation of the available amine groups leads to intermediate formation (m/z = 274). Further, m/z = 245 resulted from the cleavage of the piperazine ring after the defluorination of CIP. This then leads to the formation of m/z = 159 & 213 as further fragmentations occur upon visible light illumination in the presence of Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs. Similar results have been reported by A. Salma et al. [70] and F. Rong et al. [71] group as well under neutral pH conditions [71,72].

Based on these results, the possible reaction mechanism for the as-prepared n-n type heterojunction could be proposed, as shown in Fig. 10(b). Under visible light illumination, electrons from the Fe-MOF and N-CNDs valence bands are excited to their conduction bands, leaving holes in their respective valence bands. Since the conduction band of N-CNDs (ECB = −0.38 eV) is at a more negative level than the conduction band of Fe-MOF (ECB = −0.14 eV), the photo-generated electrons in the conduction band of N-CNDs are transferred to the conduction band of Fe-MOF. Furthermore, the photo-generated holes from the valence band of Fe-MOF ((EVB = 2.30 eV)) could be transferred to the valence band of N-CNDs ((EVB = 1.51 eV)) as it is at higher positive potential. In this system, N-CNDs act as electron donors, thus enhancing the charge separation during photoreaction by promoting radical formations which aligns well with the observed results [24,73]. This suggests the n-n heterojunction acts as a Type-II heterojunction, which prevents the recombination of charge carriers.

Further, it can be demonstrated that the electrons in the conduction band of N-CNDs are not energetic enough to significantly reduce oxygen to the superoxide radicals (E). This observation is consistent with the scavenger test results, which show a minor role of superoxide radicals, as revealed by the p-BQ test. Additionally, the holes in the valence band of Fe-MOF have a sufficiently high potential to oxidize water or hydroxide ions and generate hydroxyl radicals. These are considered highly reactive in nature and show a vital role in CIP degradation. Furthermore, holes in the valence band of Fe-MOF can directly react with CIP molecules, with N-CNDs facilitating the charge separation process in the heterojunction. The possible reaction occurring during photo-degradation of CIP using Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs under visible light irradiation could be presented as follows;

Overall, this suggests that holes and hydroxyl radicals play a significant role in CIP degradation in this study, while superoxide radicals have a minor role. Y. Ma et al.74 also proposed the hydroxyl radical-based mechanism of photocatalytic heterojunction based on Fe-MOF heterojunction, which aligns well with the scavenger test results in our study.

In this study, we successfully synthesized Fe-MOF@x%N-CNDs (x = 10, 20, 30, and 50) composites using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Then, as-prepared composites were systematically characterized and tested for ciprofloxacin degradation under visible light irradiation. Fe-MOF@30%N-CNDs photocatalyst exhibits the highest CIP removal efficiency of 97.6 %, which is 44.7 % more than the efficiency of Fe-MOF. This enhancement could be attributed to the higher surface area, visible light active bandgap, improved charge transfer properties, and reduced electron-hole recombination due to the introduction of N-CNDs in Fe-MOF. All the photodegradation studies of CIP are performed under neutral pH conditions, which is feasible for water treatment applications. Hence, this study concludes that the composition of Fe-MOF with N-CNDs could be a promising heterojunction design to improve the photocatalytic performance for ciprofloxacin removal from water.

ce:section.ce:para: Dedication.

This paper is dedicated to Professor Dionysios D. Dionysiou, who left us on November 20, 2023. Professor Dionysiou, or Dion, as he was internationally known to everyone, was a leader in numerous areas of environmental science and technology. Dion's contributions were characterized by thoroughness and great breadth. Dion will be missed very much, but his work will stay with us forever.
